topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee tasted like ash in my mouth. I watched Liam laugh across the table, a sound so easy, so effortless, and it felt like a physical blow. He was recounting a ridiculous anecdote from college, his eyes sparkling with genuine amusement. I just stared at my lukewarm mug, the ceramic digging into my palm. It wasn't just the laughter; it was the casual way he referenced shared memories—inside jokes, whispered secrets from years ago—as if I were a distant, irrelevant footnote in his life story. A tightness coiled in my chest, a dull, heavy pressure that made my breath catch in my throat. When he finally turned to me, seeking a reaction, I offered a tight, brittle smile. "That's funny," I managed, the words sounding hollow even to my own ears. I felt the familiar, unwelcome urge to retreat, to simply dissolve into the background noise of the cafe, leaving him untouched by my presence.

[story 2]

I meticulously organized the boxes in the attic, each item a tangible relic of a shared past. Dust motes danced in the single shaft of sunlight cutting through the gloom, illuminating stacks of faded photographs. There, amidst the smiling faces, was Liam. I picked up a picture of us, grinning maniacally at a poorly planned road trip. I traced the outline of my younger self in the photo, a ghost I was trying to summon back into the present. But as I looked at the picture, the warmth I expected didn't arrive. It was just a snapshot, disconnected. A strange, cold knot formed low in my stomach. I dropped the photo, the sound echoing unnaturally loud in the silence. I didn't want to talk about it. I certainly didn't want to try.

[story 3]

"Remember that time we tried to build that ridiculous fort?" Liam asked, leaning back in his chair, his expression bright and unguarded. He was talking about a summer afternoon, a moment that, to me, was the bedrock of our entire friendship. I nodded, a small, jerky movement. Inside, a fierce, defensive wall was rising. Every word he spoke about that time felt like a deliberate exclusion. I watched his eyes light up, completely absorbed in the narrative, and I felt a distinct hardening around my jaw. I focused intensely on the pattern of the wooden floorboards beneath my worn sneakers, trying to anchor myself to something solid, anything, because the air around him felt suddenly vast and empty of my significance.
